title: Bidirectional Language Models Are Also Few-shot Learners

structured.summary: This paper introduces Sequential Autoregressive Prompting (SAP), a novel method that enables bidirectional language models like mT5 to perform few-shot and zero-shot prompting by iteratively generating one token at a time. SAP unlocks strong prompt-based learning capabilities in bidirectional models, achieving state-of-the-art results on multilingual machine translation, question answering, and summarization tasks, often outperforming larger unidirectional models without fine-tuning.

strengths.point: Innovative prompting technique enabling autoregressive generation in bidirectional models
strengths.evidence: SAP iteratively feeds back the first generated token per iteration, effectively turning masked span prediction models into autoregressive generators at the encoder-decoder level (Section 3).

strengths.point: Strong empirical results across diverse tasks and languages
strengths.evidence: mT5 with SAP outperforms larger unidirectional models GPT-3 and XGLM on 182 language pairs in few-shot and zero-shot MT, and shows gains on QA (XQuAD, SQuAD) and summarization (CNN/Daily Mail) benchmarks (Section 5).

strengths.point: Effective unsupervised zero-shot MT bootstrapping with filtering and prompt ensembling
strengths.evidence: The paper proposes synthetic data filtering using mT5Score, prompt ensembling, and English-centric bootstrapping to improve zero-shot MT, surpassing few-shot baselines and prior zero-shot methods (Section 4.3, 5.2).

strengths.point: Demonstrates that prompt-based few-shot and zero-shot learning is an emergent property of bidirectional models
strengths.evidence: SAP enables bidirectional models to perform prompt-based learning without fine-tuning, challenging the assumption that only unidirectional models are promptable (Introduction, Discussion).

weaknesses.point: Insufficient comparison to prior iterative decoding and span-based generation methods
weaknesses.evidence: Related work does not explicitly compare SAP to methods like Mask-Predict or Levenshtein Transformer, which also generate outputs iteratively or condition on partial outputs (Section 2).

weaknesses.point: Lack of ablations on token generation granularity and prompt sensitivity
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments explore generating multiple tokens per iteration or analyze robustness to prompt example selection and ordering (Section 3.1, 5).

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation of computational cost and scalability
weaknesses.evidence: The paper notes SAP requires multiple forward passes proportional to output length but does not provide quantitative latency or resource usage comparisons (Section 6).

weaknesses.point: Uncontrolled baseline comparisons confounded by architectural and data differences
weaknesses.evidence: Comparisons between mT5 (encoder-decoder) and GPT-3/XGLM (decoder-only) differ in model architecture, parameter count, and pre-training data, complicating attribution of gains to SAP (Section 5).

weaknesses.point: Missing statistical significance testing and variance reporting
weaknesses.evidence: Results tables report only point estimates without confidence intervals or multiple runs, limiting confidence in consistent improvements (Tables 2, 4, 5).
Important implementation details such as prompt formatting, decoding hyperparameters, random seeds, and synthetic data filtering thresholds are not fully specified (Sections 3, 4, Appendix).

weaknesses.point: Limited generalizability beyond mT5 and selected tasks
weaknesses.evidence: SAP is only evaluated on mT5 and three tasks; applicability to other bidirectional models or generation domains remains untested (Section 6).

weaknesses.point: No human evaluation or automatic hallucination metrics to support claims on output quality
weaknesses.evidence: Quality claims rely on standard metrics and anecdotal examples without systematic human annotation or hallucination-specific evaluation (Section 5.5).

questions.question: How does SAP compare to existing iterative decoding or span-based generation methods in terms of novelty and performance?
questions.motivation: Clarifying SAP’s distinct contributions relative to prior iterative generation approaches would strengthen understanding of its innovation.

questions.question: What are the practical trade-offs between output quality and computational efficiency when generating multiple tokens per iteration versus single-token SAP?
questions.motivation: Understanding these trade-offs is critical for deploying SAP in real-world applications with latency constraints.

questions.question: Can SAP be effectively applied to other bidirectional models with different architectures or pre-training objectives?
questions.motivation: Demonstrating generalizability beyond mT5 would broaden SAP’s impact and guide future model design.

questions.question: How sensitive is SAP’s performance to prompt design, example selection, and ordering in few-shot settings?
questions.motivation: Prompt robustness analysis is important for reliable application and reproducibility of results.

questions.question: What is the extent of error accumulation or exposure bias in SAP’s iterative single-token generation?
questions.motivation: Analyzing error propagation can inform improvements and limitations of SAP’s iterative decoding.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient comparison to prior iterative decoding and span-based generation methods. Related work does not explicitly compare SAP to methods like Mask-Predict or Levenshtein Transformer, which also generate outputs iteratively or condition on partial outputs (Section 2)
critique_points.point_002: Lack of ablations on token generation granularity and prompt sensitivity. No experiments explore generating multiple tokens per iteration or analyze robustness to prompt example selection and ordering (Section 3.1, 5)
critique_points.point_003: Limited evaluation of computational cost and scalability. The paper notes SAP requires multiple forward passes proportional to output length but does not provide quantitative latency or resource usage comparisons (Section 6)
critique_points.point_004: Uncontrolled baseline comparisons confounded by architectural and data differences. Comparisons between mT5 (encoder-decoder) and GPT-3/XGLM (decoder-only) differ in model architecture, parameter count, and pre-training data, complicating attribution of gains to SAP (Section 5)
critique_points.point_005: Missing statistical significance testing and variance reporting. Results tables report only point estimates without confidence intervals or multiple runs, limiting confidence in consistent improvements (Tables 2, 4, 5)
critique_points.point_006: Incomplete reproducibility details. Important implementation details such as prompt formatting, decoding hyperparameters, random seeds, and synthetic data filtering thresholds are not fully specified (Sections 3, 4, Appendix)
critique_points.point_007: Limited generalizability beyond mT5 and selected tasks. SAP is only evaluated on mT5 and three tasks; applicability to other bidirectional models or generation domains remains untested (Section 6)
critique_points.point_008: No human evaluation or automatic hallucination metrics to support claims on output quality. Quality claims rely on standard metrics and anecdotal examples without systematic human annotation or hallucination-specific evaluation (Section 5.5)
